title: Ingestion Queue
summary: This queue defines the recommended order for deeper ingestion. Each pass should read the repository itself, capture the important files into raw/ as needed, and then create or expand the corresponding wiki pages.
text: ingestion queue this queue defines the recommended order for deeper ingestion. each pass should read the repository itself, capture the important files into raw/ as needed, and then create or expand the corresponding wiki pages. # ingestion queue this queue defines the recommended order for deeper ingestion. each pass should read the repository itself, capture the important files into raw/ as needed, and then create or expand the corresponding wiki pages. ## recommended order 1. sem-4-iitb-ee -> seed page created 2. sem-5-iitb-ee -> seed page created 3. sem-6-iitb-ee -> seed page created 4. sem-7-iitb-ee -> seed page created 5. sem-8-iitb-ee -> seed page created 6. soc-2020 -> seed page created 7. umic_team4-final -> seed page created 8. instishop -> target page wiki/projects/instishop.md 9. ma-tuts -> seed page created 10. mooc-content -> seed page created 11. reports_soc2020 -> seed page created 12. 16-bit-alu -> seed page created 13. autumn-of-automation -> seed page created 14. iamprasann.github.io -> intentionally skipped because this repository is the host knowledge base ## why this order - start with the semester repositories because they are likely to expose the broadest undergraduate structure. - move next to bounded projects that can become stronger narrative pages. - leave forks and support repositories later, once the main academic story is already mapped. - do not ingest the site repository itself; it is the host knowledge base for the derived wiki. ## per-repository pass checklist - read readme and top-level structure. - identify courses, projects, reports, notebooks, presentations, and datasets. - capture important references, screenshots, and source links into raw/ only when needed. - write or expand the project page in wiki/projects/. - update wiki/indexes/repositories.md with the local page link once it exists. - create follow-on concept, course, or timeline pages when the repo supports broader synthesis. ## related - repositories ## sources - raw/github/2026-04-21-iamprasann-repositories.json indexes

title: Repositories

title: 16-bit-ALU
summary: 16-bit-ALU is a forked but still meaningful hardware-design repository for an EE224 course project from 2020. The repository is small, flat, and focused: a structural VHDL implementation of a 16-bit ALU, built from lower-level gate and arithmetic modules, with a dedicated testbench and project artifacts.
text: 16-bit-alu 16-bit-alu is a forked but still meaningful hardware-design repository for an ee224 course project from 2020. the repository is small, flat, and focused: a structural vhdl implementation of a 16-bit alu, built from lower-level gate and arithmetic modules, with a dedicated testbench and project artifacts. # 16-bit-alu 16-bit-alu is a forked but still meaningful hardware-design repository for an ee224 course project from 2020. the repository is small, flat, and focused: a structural vhdl implementation of a 16-bit alu, built from lower-level gate and arithmetic modules, with a dedicated testbench and project artifacts. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/16-bit-alu - parent repository: vineetgala/16-bit-alu - created: december 5, 2020 - updated: december 5, 2020 - primary language on github: vhdl - fork: yes the root readme is minimal, but it clearly identifies the repo as a 16 bit alu for ee224 course project in 2020. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it adds a hardware-design thread to the knowledge base. it is not just a report or a simulation notebook; it preserves the actual vhdl design units, the structural top-level alu composition, and a testbench that exercises many input patterns. even though the repo is forked, it still reflects real work on: - digital design decomposition into reusable modules - arithmetic and logic block composition - alu control-path selection through multiplexing - testbench-driven verification that makes it a valid part of the undergraduate engineering record. ## how it worked the top-level alu.vhd defines two select inputs, sixteen-bit operands a and b exposed as individual bit ports, sixteen result bits, a carry output, and a zero flag. internally, the architecture instantiates separate modules for xor, nand, addition, and subtraction, computes those candidate outputs in parallel, and then selects among them using sixteen mux_4to1 components controlled by s1 and s0. the carry line is also selected through a mux, and a full_zero component derives the zero flag from the output result. in other words, the alu is implemented as a structural composition of independent logic/arithmetic units rather than a single behavioral block. the repo also contains supporting modules such as: - full_adder.vhd - full_subtract.vhd - full_xor.vhd - full_nand.vhd - full_zero.vhd - lower-level helper units like my_and.vhd, my_or.vhd, my_not.vhd, my_xor.vhd, and related files testbench.vhd applies a large number of explicit bit-pattern test cases across the different select modes, which shows that validation was built into the project workflow. ## key artifacts - root readme - alu.vhd - testbench.vhd - project-parta.pdf - finalgraph.png representative artifacts visible in the repo include: - top-level alu composition in vhdl - supporting gate and arithmetic modules - a large explicit testbench - project/report images and pdf documentation ## lessons learned on a first pass, 16-bit-alu is best understood as a compact digital-design project archive. its main value is not scale but clarity: the repo shows the structure of the design, the decomposition into modules, and the existence of verification work. the clearest second-pass opportunities are: - a deeper page on the alu architecture and operation-selection logic - a closer reading of the project pdf and images to recover the original design rationale and results ## related - autumn-of-automation - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/16-bit-alu - 16-bit-alu readme - alu.vhd - testbench.vhd forked contributions

title: Autumn-of-Automation
summary: Autumn-of-Automation is a forked training-program submission repository, but it is still meaningful evidence of work done because the fork itself was the intended delivery mechanism. The repo is organized by topic tracks such as Python, OpenCV, ROS, Gazebo, and ML, and preserves submitted exercises across those areas.
text: autumn-of-automation autumn-of-automation is a forked training-program submission repository, but it is still meaningful evidence of work done because the fork itself was the intended delivery mechanism. the repo is organized by topic tracks such as python, opencv, ros, gazebo, and ml, and preserves submitted exercises across those areas. # autumn-of-automation autumn-of-automation is a forked training-program submission repository, but it is still meaningful evidence of work done because the fork itself was the intended delivery mechanism. the repo is organized by topic tracks such as python, opencv, ros, gazebo, and ml, and preserves submitted exercises across those areas. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/autumn-of-automation - parent repository: innovation-cell/autumn-of-automation - created: july 21, 2020 - updated: august 4, 2020 - primary language on github: python - fork: yes top-level structure: - python - opencv - ros - gazebo - ml the root readme makes the intended workflow explicit: participants were supposed to fork the upstream repo and submit their assignments in their own fork. that means the fork status is contextual, not disqualifying. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it captures hands-on training work across several technical tracks in one place. even though it is not a standalone original codebase, it documents actual contribution and completion in: - introductory python exercises - computer-vision tasks in opencv - ros message/script exercises - at least one machine-learning notebook assignment that makes it useful as evidence of early breadth and practical experimentation. ## how it worked the repo is organized by topic rather than by a single project narrative. - python contains a sequence of introductory scripts plus a text file artifact, which suggests early programming exercises. - opencv is the richest area, with multiple numbered tasks, supporting images/xml, and generated outputs. it appears to preserve the main vision-oriented assignment work. - ros contains message definitions, a quaternion-to-euler script, movement/rotation scripts, and a solution image, pointing to robotics middleware exercises. - gazebo currently contains only a placeholder file, so it looks like a reserved track with no substantial preserved submission in this fork. - ml contains a single notebook, sunglassdetectorcnn.ipynb, which likely represents a later-stage machine-learning assignment in the program. the first-pass interpretation is that this repo is best read as a training-work archive with several small practical tracks rather than one cohesive software project. ## key artifacts - root readme - python - opencv - ros - ml representative artifacts visible in the repo include: - a sequence of firstprogram.py through fifthprogram.py - opencv task scripts and supporting image assets - ros message definitions and movement scripts - sunglassdetectorcnn.ipynb ## lessons learned on a first pass, autumn-of-automation should not be treated like a fully original standalone project, but it should also not be dismissed just because it is forked. the upstream workflow explicitly required forks for assignment submission, so this repo is a valid record of personal work within a shared training framework. the clearest second-pass opportunities are: - a deeper page for the opencv track, since it contains the most substantial preserved work - a brief concept page linking this training repo to later ros, cv, or ml work elsewhere in the corpus ## related - umic_team4-final - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/autumn-of-automation - autumn-of-automation readme forked contributions

title: MA-tuts
summary: MA-tuts is a compact academic-support repository rather than a software project. It collects tutorial material across three mathematics courses, MA106, MA109, and MA205, and appears to function as a personal archive of chapter notes, weekly exercises, tutorial sheets, and worked solutions.
text: ma-tuts ma-tuts is a compact academic-support repository rather than a software project. it collects tutorial material across three mathematics courses, ma106, ma109, and ma205, and appears to function as a personal archive of chapter notes, weekly exercises, tutorial sheets, and worked solutions. # ma-tuts ma-tuts is a compact academic-support repository rather than a software project. it collects tutorial material across three mathematics courses, ma106, ma109, and ma205, and appears to function as a personal archive of chapter notes, weekly exercises, tutorial sheets, and worked solutions. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/ma-tuts - created: september 6, 2023 - updated: september 6, 2023 - primary language on github: none identified top-level course folders: - ma106 - ma109 - ma205 the root readme is minimal, so the meaning of the repo comes almost entirely from the course folders and document names. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it broadens the knowledge base beyond engineering and coding artifacts. it preserves a mathematical study layer that supports the undergraduate story in a different way: - chapter-based conceptual material in ma106 - weekly exercise/tutorial material in ma109 - a much fuller tutorial-and-solutions archive in ma205 that makes the repo useful as evidence of how tutorial work and mathematical preparation were organized outside conventional coding projects. ## how it worked the repository is organized directly by course. - ma106 is structured around topic pdfs such as matrices, determinants, vector spaces, linear transformations, and eigenvectors, plus a tutorial-sheet file. - ma109 is organized around weekly pdfs and an exercise file, suggesting a simple tutorial workflow. - ma205 is the most extensive folder, with multiple tutorial pdfs, corresponding solution pdfs, a final tutorial document, and a nested onenote-style folder for complex analysis tutorials. this makes ma-tuts feel less like a project repository and more like a curated working archive for solving and reviewing mathematics tutorial content over time. ## key artifacts - root readme - ma106 - ma109 - ma205 representative artifacts visible in the repo include: - chapter-wise pdfs and tutorial sheets in ma106 - weekly exercise/tutorial pdfs in ma109 - tutorial pdfs and matching solution pdfs in ma205 - .one tutorial files in ma205 - complex analysis (t6) ## lessons learned on a first pass, ma-tuts reads as a useful support repository rather than a centerpiece project. it is still worth ingesting because it documents the academic scaffolding around the rest of the undergraduate work, especially for mathematically intensive courses. the clearest second-pass opportunities are: - a course page for ma205 if the math side of the knowledge base needs more depth - a concept page linking the linear algebra and complex analysis material to later engineering or machine-learning work where relevant ## related - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/ma-tuts - ma-tuts readme coursework & practice

title: MOOC-content
summary: MOOC-content is a self-study repository that archives practice and assignments from major online courses rather than institutional coursework. It combines algorithmic programming assignments, the full Andrew Ng machine-learning exercise sequence, and a Deep Learning Specialization completion artifact. In the knowledge base, it reads as a record of structured learning outside the formal undergraduate curriculum.
text: mooc-content mooc-content is a self-study repository that archives practice and assignments from major online courses rather than institutional coursework. it combines algorithmic programming assignments, the full andrew ng machine-learning exercise sequence, and a deep learning specialization completion artifact. in the knowledge base, it reads as a record of structured learning outside the formal undergraduate curriculum. # mooc-content mooc-content is a self-study repository that archives practice and assignments from major online courses rather than institutional coursework. it combines algorithmic programming assignments, the full andrew ng machine-learning exercise sequence, and a deep learning specialization completion artifact. in the knowledge base, it reads as a record of structured learning outside the formal undergraduate curriculum. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/mooc-content - created: july 21, 2021 - updated: july 21, 2021 - primary language on github: matlab top-level structure: - dsa practice assignments - machine learning - andrew ng - deep learning specialization certificate.pdf the root readme explicitly links the coursera sources, so the repo already explains itself better than many of the other archival repositories. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it captures the self-directed learning layer behind the rest of the undergraduate work. it shows deliberate work on: - data structures and algorithms implementation practice in c++ - classical machine-learning assignments in matlab/octave - the broader deep-learning learning path, at least at the certificate/completion level that makes it useful evidence of preparation and breadth outside formal course repositories. ## how it worked the repo is organized by mooc track rather than by semester or project. - dsa practice assignments contains three major specialization folders: algorithmic toolbox, algorithms on graphs, and data structures. - the recursive file listing shows week-by-week pdfs plus many c++ solutions spanning warmup problems, greedy algorithms, divide-and-conquer, dynamic programming, graph decomposition, shortest paths, mst, hash tables, bsts, and related problems. - machine learning - andrew ng contains machine-learning-ex1 through machine-learning-ex8, which matches the full exercise sequence of the classic course. - those folders preserve the standard assignment pdfs, datasets, helper code, and .m solutions for topics like regression, classification, neural networks, bias/variance, svms, clustering, pca, anomaly detection, and recommender systems. - the root-level deep-learning certificate pdf preserves completion evidence for the deep learning specialization even though the underlying assignment code is not stored here. taken together, the repo looks like a disciplined archive of online-course execution rather than a single end-to-end project. ## key artifacts - root readme - dsa practice assignments - machine learning - andrew ng - deep learning specialization certificate.pdf representative artifacts visible in the repo include: - weekly dsa assignment pdfs and c++ implementations - eight matlab/octave machine-learning exercise sets with datasets and helper files - a deep-learning specialization certificate ## lessons learned on a first pass, mooc-content reads as a strong support repository rather than a narrative project repo. its value comes from what it demonstrates: systematic practice, breadth across algorithms and ml, and external learning that likely fed into later coursework and projects. the clearest second-pass opportunities are: - a concept page linking the dsa practice to later systems or algorithm-heavy work - a concept page linking the andrew ng ml exercises to later machine-learning projects in the corpus ## related - ma-tuts - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/mooc-content - mooc-content readme coursework & practice

title: reports_SOC2020
summary: reports_SOC2020 is a minimal document-only repository that appears to serve as a report archive related to SOC-2020. Unlike SOC-2020, which preserves the task structure, notebooks, code, datasets, and group folders, this repo contains only a small set of top-level PDF outputs and no README.
text: reports_soc2020 reports_soc2020 is a minimal document-only repository that appears to serve as a report archive related to soc-2020. unlike soc-2020, which preserves the task structure, notebooks, code, datasets, and group folders, this repo contains only a small set of top-level pdf outputs and no readme. # reports_soc2020 reports_soc2020 is a minimal document-only repository that appears to serve as a report archive related to soc-2020. unlike soc-2020, which preserves the task structure, notebooks, code, datasets, and group folders, this repo contains only a small set of top-level pdf outputs and no readme. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/reports_soc2020 - created: june 2, 2020 - updated: june 2, 2020 - primary language on github: none identified top-level files: - assignment4_2_la (3).pdf - assignment4_3_improved__la (1).pdf - assignment5_la (1).pdf - neural_networks (1).pdf - teamla_report (4).pdf there is no readme, so the meaning of the repo has to be inferred from the filenames and its relationship to soc-2020. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it preserves a simpler artifact layer than soc-2020: the report outputs themselves, stripped away from the surrounding notebooks, code, and datasets. that makes it useful as a companion archive even if it is not a strong standalone project repo. it also helps clarify that some work in the corpus exists in two forms: - as full working/project folders in soc-2020 - as flattened report outputs in reports_soc2020 ## how it worked on first pass, reports_soc2020 appears to be a flat export of key report pdfs rather than a repository designed for ongoing development. the filenames overlap with artifacts already seen during the soc-2020 ingestion, especially: - teamla_report (4).pdf - neural_networks (1).pdf - assignment4_2_la (3).pdf - assignment4_3_improved__la (1).pdf that overlap strongly suggests this repo is better interpreted as a report shelf or snapshot derived from the broader soc-2020 workflow rather than as an independent body of work. ## key artifacts - repository root - assignment4_2_la (3).pdf - assignment4_3_improved__la (1).pdf - assignment5_la (1).pdf - neural_networks (1).pdf - teamla_report (4).pdf ## lessons learned on a first pass, reports_soc2020 is best treated as a support/archive repository rather than a primary narrative repo. its main value is as corroborating evidence for soc-2020, not as a separate project line. the clearest second-pass opportunity is not to deepen this repo in isolation, but to cross-link its pdfs back into the relevant soc-2020 subprojects where they belong contextually. ## related - soc-2020 - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/reports_soc2020 coursework & practice

title: SEM-4-IITB-EE
summary: SEM-4-IITB-EE is a semester repository that consolidates coursework from Semester 4 at IIT Bombay Electrical Engineering. On first inspection, it functions less like a single project and more like a semester container: one place for assignments, lab work, question papers, and at least one course project across five different courses.
text: sem-4-iitb-ee sem-4-iitb-ee is a semester repository that consolidates coursework from semester 4 at iit bombay electrical engineering. on first inspection, it functions less like a single project and more like a semester container: one place for assignments, lab work, question papers, and at least one course project across five different courses. # sem-4-iitb-ee sem-4-iitb-ee is a semester repository that consolidates coursework from semester 4 at iit bombay electrical engineering. on first inspection, it functions less like a single project and more like a semester container: one place for assignments, lab work, question papers, and at least one course project across five different courses. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/sem-4-iitb-ee - created: may 19, 2021 - primary language on github: jupyter notebook - top-level course folders: cs213m, cs736, ee214, ee309, ee337 the repo readme frames it as "all the unnecessarily excessive coding" done in the semester, which fits the structure: it is a broad evidence repository for semester work rather than a narrowly scoped codebase. ## why it mattered this repository matters to the knowledge base because it exposes the shape of an entire semester instead of a single artifact. it is the kind of source that can later support: - course pages - a semester timeline - concept pages around digital circuits, microprocessors, algorithms, and medical image computing - project pages for standout subprojects that deserve their own treatment ## how it worked the repository is organized by course, and each course folder appears to preserve the actual working structure of the semester rather than a polished retrospective. - cs213m contains five assignments and a readme that links question papers or hackerrank contests for each assignment. - cs736 contains three assignments plus a course project, with the readme linking image denoising, image segmentation, shape analysis, and the course project presentation. - ee214 appears to be digital circuits lab work, including experiments, midsem and endsem material, an fsm folder, and a four-bit adder folder. - ee309 contains four microprocessor assignments. - ee337 is organized week by week and includes a project tapcode folder; its readme also links to the public course overview and tools pages. ## key artifacts - root readme - cs213m - cs736 - ee214 - ee309 - ee337 - cs213m readme - cs736 readme - ee337 readme ## lessons learned on this first pass, the strongest lesson is structural: semester repositories like this are high-value knowledge sources because they preserve the context around the work, not just the final submissions. even without reading every notebook yet, the repo already shows a habit of keeping assignments, lab material, project artifacts, and problem statements close together, which makes later synthesis much easier. there is also a clear next step for deeper ingestion: this repo should likely be split outward into course pages and possibly subproject pages after the individual folders are read in more detail. ## related - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/sem-4-iitb-ee - sem-4 readme - cs213m readme - cs736 readme - ee337 readme semester archives

title: SEM-5-IITB-EE
summary: SEM-5-IITB-EE is a semester repository for Semester 5 coursework at IIT Bombay Electrical Engineering. Compared with SEM-4-IITB-EE, this repo looks less like a broad mix of code and more like a structured archive of reports, lab manuals, prelabs, problem sheets, and course submissions, with one especially notable project nested inside CS663.
text: sem-5-iitb-ee sem-5-iitb-ee is a semester repository for semester 5 coursework at iit bombay electrical engineering. compared with sem-4-iitb-ee, this repo looks less like a broad mix of code and more like a structured archive of reports, lab manuals, prelabs, problem sheets, and course submissions, with one especially notable project nested inside cs663. # sem-5-iitb-ee sem-5-iitb-ee is a semester repository for semester 5 coursework at iit bombay electrical engineering. compared with sem-4-iitb-ee, this repo looks less like a broad mix of code and more like a structured archive of reports, lab manuals, prelabs, problem sheets, and course submissions, with one especially notable project nested inside cs663. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/sem-5-iitb-ee - created: september 4, 2021 - updated: november 18, 2021 - primary language on github: python - top-level course folders: cs663, ee230, ee324, ee340 the root readme is minimal, so most of the semester story has to be reconstructed from the folder structure and artifact types. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it preserves the working record of a semester in a different mode from the previous one. here the knowledge is carried less by polished top-level explanations and more by the density of the artifacts themselves: - assignment reports - lab writeups - prelab material - manuals and problem sheets - one research-style project with explicit paper references that makes this repo a strong source for later course pages and for tracing how academic work was documented and submitted. ## how it worked the semester is divided into four course-code folders, each with a distinct rhythm. - cs663 contains two assignment groups plus a fingerprint recognition project. - ee230 is organized as expt-0 through expt-5, suggesting a lab progression. - ee324 is organized as lab 1 through lab 5, each paired with problem sheets and submitted pdfs. - ee340 runs from week 1 through week 9, with repeated prelab and lab sheet patterns. the most distinctive sub-area is the cs663/fingerprint recognition project folder. its readme describes the code as walking-to-sp, a matlab implementation for detecting fingerprint singular points. it cites a 2016 pattern recognition paper and a related 2016 neural computing & applications paper, and it notes a dependency on peter kovesi's matlab computer vision functions. that makes this folder the clearest bridge between coursework and research-style implementation in the repository. ## key artifacts - root readme - cs663 - ee230 - ee324 - ee340 - fingerprint project readme - fingerprint project report representative artifact types visible in the repository include: - assignment report pdfs in cs663 - experiment sheets and submitted reports in ee230 - problem sheets and submission pdfs in ee324 - weekly prelab and lab manuals in ee340 ## lessons learned on a first pass, this repository shows a more archival style of academic work than sem-4-iitb-ee. the repo is less self-explanatory, but still rich: the combination of repeated lab artifacts and one clearly named project makes it useful both as evidence of coursework volume and as a source for more focused follow-up pages. the most important next step here is to split the semester outward in two directions: - course pages for cs663, ee230, ee324, and ee340 - a dedicated page for the fingerprint recognition project, which already has a narrative readme, a report, and cited references ## related - sem-4-iitb-ee - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/sem-5-iitb-ee - sem-5 readme - cs663 fingerprint project readme semester archives

title: SEM-6-IITB-EE
summary: SEM-6-IITB-EE is a semester repository for Semester 6 coursework at IIT Bombay Electrical Engineering. At first pass, it looks more advanced and more project-oriented than the earlier semester repos: the course set spans operating systems, hardware design, technical communication, radar systems, communication networks, and machine learning, and several folders preserve both source material and final deliverables.
text: sem-6-iitb-ee sem-6-iitb-ee is a semester repository for semester 6 coursework at iit bombay electrical engineering. at first pass, it looks more advanced and more project-oriented than the earlier semester repos: the course set spans operating systems, hardware design, technical communication, radar systems, communication networks, and machine learning, and several folders preserve both source material and final deliverables. # sem-6-iitb-ee sem-6-iitb-ee is a semester repository for semester 6 coursework at iit bombay electrical engineering. at first pass, it looks more advanced and more project-oriented than the earlier semester repos: the course set spans operating systems, hardware design, technical communication, radar systems, communication networks, and machine learning, and several folders preserve both source material and final deliverables. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/sem-6-iitb-ee - created: september 6, 2023 - updated: september 6, 2023 - primary language on github: jupyter notebook top-level course folders: - cs347m - operating systems - ee344 - electronic design lab - ee350 - technical communication - ee602 - radar systems - ee706 - communication networks - ee769 - machine learning the root readme is minimal, so the semester narrative has to be reconstructed from the artifact structure and file names. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it captures a semester where the work appears to broaden in both technical range and output format. instead of mainly weekly lab sheets or assignment bundles, the repo shows: - systems work with reports and reference material - hardware design documentation and proposal/final-report structure - seminar-style communication deliverables - a radar project with source readings and a typeset report - machine learning notebooks, scripts, data, and personal notes that mix makes the repo valuable not only as a semester archive but also as a source for later concept and course pages. ## how it worked each course folder has its own mode of work. - title: SEM-7-IITB-EE
summary: SEM-7-IITB-EE is a compact semester repository that differs sharply from the earlier semester archives. Instead of many course folders, it contains one focused course area, EE 6103 - Advanced Network Security, and a separate placements directory. That makes it less of a conventional semester log and more of a semester snapshot organized around one advanced academic thread plus career-preparation material.
text: sem-7-iitb-ee sem-7-iitb-ee is a compact semester repository that differs sharply from the earlier semester archives. instead of many course folders, it contains one focused course area, ee 6103 - title: SEM-8-IITB-EE
summary: SEM-8-IITB-EE is a final-semester repository with a tighter, more elective profile than the earlier semester archives. Instead of broad multi-course accumulation, it captures four advanced areas: biomedical microsystems, research and development, restructured power systems, and cryptology. The overall impression is of a semester centered on higher-level project work, research material, and specialized technical topics.
text: sem-8-iitb-ee sem-8-iitb-ee is a final-semester repository with a tighter, more elective profile than the earlier semester archives. instead of broad multi-course accumulation, it captures four advanced areas: biomedical microsystems, research and development, restructured power systems, and cryptology. the overall impression is of a semester centered on higher-level project work, research material, and specialized technical topics. # sem-8-iitb-ee sem-8-iitb-ee is a final-semester repository with a tighter, more elective profile than the earlier semester archives. instead of broad multi-course accumulation, it captures four advanced areas: biomedical microsystems, research and development, restructured power systems, and cryptology. the overall impression is of a semester centered on higher-level project work, research material, and specialized technical topics. ## overview - title: SOC-2020
summary: SOC-2020 is a compact but well-structured archive of Summer of Code style work organized under the project label Intelligent Agents. Unlike the semester repositories, it already exposes a clear learning sequence in the README: start with foundational tasks, move into individual simulations, then build toward group projects and a neural-networks track.
text: soc-2020 soc-2020 is a compact but well-structured archive of summer of code style work organized under the project label intelligent agents. unlike the semester repositories, it already exposes a clear learning sequence in the readme: start with foundational tasks, move into individual simulations, then build toward group projects and a neural-networks track. # soc-2020 soc-2020 is a compact but well-structured archive of summer of code style work organized under the project label intelligent agents. unlike the semester repositories, it already exposes a clear learning sequence in the readme: start with foundational tasks, move into individual simulations, then build toward group projects and a neural-networks track. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/soc-2020 - created: june 4, 2020 - updated: june 4, 2020 - primary language on github: jupyter notebook - main working path: prasann files/soc-2020 the root readme provides the strongest narrative framing of any repo ingested so far. it explicitly names the project as intelligent agents and lays out the sequence of tasks and projects. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it preserves a program-like progression rather than just a pile of submissions. it captures: - an initial r-based simulation task - notebook-based follow-up work - individual simulation exercises in one and two dimensions - group projects on pandemic tracing, regression, and music prediction - a separate neural-networks individual project that makes it a useful early anchor in the knowledge base for showing how technical work evolved from guided tasks into more open-ended project work. ## how it worked the repo is organized around successive stages of work inside prasann files/soc-2020. - task 1 contains a problem statement pdf and an r script for dice simulation. - task 2 moves into a notebook-based approach to the next assignment. - indvdl 1 and indvdl 2 collect notebook-based simulations in one-dimensional and two-dimensional settings. - indvdl 3 shifts toward neural-network material with notebooks and a supporting pdf. - grp 1 appears to extend the earlier spread-simulation work into a pandemic-tracing project with python code, report pdfs, and csv data. - grp 2 applies regression to the admission_predict dataset. - grp 3 develops a music predictor system using notebooks, csv data, and python scripts. the most revealing nested note is in grp 3/readme.md, which describes post-submission iteration beyond the original report. it records a progression from running_project.py to updated-running.py, with the explicit goal of reducing repeated songs and improving the final pattern interpreter. that makes grp 3 the clearest example of an iterative project rather than a one-shot assignment submission. ## key artifacts - root readme - task 1 - task 2 - indvdl 1 - indvdl 2 - indvdl 3 - grp 1 - grp 2 - grp 3 - grp 3 readme representative artifact types visible in the repo include: - an r script and problem statement pdf in task 1 - jupyter notebooks across the task and individual folders - python code, reports, and csv data in the group-project folders - a neural-networks pdf plus notebooks in indvdl 3 ## lessons learned on a first pass, soc-2020 looks like one of the clearest early-learning repositories in the corpus. the folder structure makes the educational progression legible, and the combination of individual and group work gives it more narrative shape than a typical assignment repo. the clearest second-pass opportunities are: - a dedicated page for the intelligent agents progression as a whole - a dedicated page for the pandemic-tracing work in grp 1 - a dedicated page for the music predictor system in grp 3 ## related - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/soc-2020 - soc-2020 readme - grp 3 readme standalone projects

title: UMIC_TEAM4-Final
summary: UMIC_TEAM4-Final is a ROS/Gazebo robotics project preserved as the final setup for a UMIC recruitment project. Unlike the semester archives and notebook-heavy coursework repos, this repository is clearly a workspace-oriented engineering project: package structure, launch files, URDF assets, controller configs, environment worlds, and Python scripts for perception and control.
text: umic_team4-final umic_team4-final is a ros/gazebo robotics project preserved as the final setup for a umic recruitment project. unlike the semester archives and notebook-heavy coursework repos, this repository is clearly a workspace-oriented engineering project: package structure, launch files, urdf assets, controller configs, environment worlds, and python scripts for perception and control. # umic_team4-final umic_team4-final is a ros/gazebo robotics project preserved as the final setup for a umic recruitment project. unlike the semester archives and notebook-heavy coursework repos, this repository is clearly a workspace-oriented engineering project: package structure, launch files, urdf assets, controller configs, environment worlds, and python scripts for perception and control. ## overview - repository: iamprasann/umic_team4-final - created: august 22, 2020 - updated: august 23, 2020 - primary language on github: cmake top-level structure: - src - src_aryan - readme.md the root readme frames the repository as the final setup for the umic recruitment project and explicitly calls out ball_detection as a working component. ## why it mattered this repository matters because it looks like one of the earliest fully engineering-shaped projects in the corpus. it is not just a set of assignments or reports; it captures: - a real package-oriented robotics workspace - simulation and visualization setup via ros/gazebo tooling - perception work through ball_detection.py - robot-description and control configuration assets - an alternate or earlier workspace tree preserved alongside the final one that makes it important as evidence of hands-on systems integration work. ## how it worked the main final workspace lives under src, where two ros packages, final_description and final_gazebo, define the robot and simulation environment. - final_description contains launch files for controls, gazebo, rviz, flaps, gates, and differential drive. - it also includes yaml controller/joint configuration, urdf/xacro assets, and python scripts such as ball_detection.py, pushing_ball.py, and laser_distance.py. - final_gazebo contains the gazebo launch file and world definition. the readme describes the intended setup as a catkin workflow: create a workspace, build it, adjust the package setup, relaunch, and then run the ball-detection script. the readme also notes that the ball-detection path returns center coordinates relative to a 400x400 camera frame, which makes the vision component a concrete project milestone rather than just a placeholder script. the src_aryan directory preserves a second ros workspace layout with packages like bot1, env, and signboard packages. that tree looks like an earlier or parallel version of the same project, and it is useful context because it shows the project was not a single flat code drop but evolved through multiple package arrangements. ## key artifacts - root readme - src/final_description - src/final_gazebo - src_aryan/bot1 - src_aryan/env representative artifacts visible in the repo include: - ros package manifests and cmakelists.txt - launch files for controls, gazebo, rviz, gates, flaps, and differential drive - yaml configuration files for robot joints and mechanisms - urdf, xacro, and gazebo description assets - python scripts for ball detection, image handling, laser distance, and ball pushing - world files and signboard/environment packages in the alternate workspace ## lessons learned on a first pass, umic_team4-final reads as a strong early systems-integration project. the repo preserves both the simulation scaffolding and the perception/control logic, which is more valuable than a polished report alone because it shows how the project was actually assembled. the clearest second-pass opportunities are: - a dedicated page for the ball_detection and perception workflow - a dedicated page for the robot/simulation architecture across final_description and final_gazebo - a note explaining the relationship between src and src_aryan ## related - repositories - ingestion queue ## sources - local capture - iamprasann/umic_team4-final - umic_team4-final readme standalone projects
